text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY - FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Approximately 6 months post-completion of definitive concurrent chemoradiation

ATTENDING ONCOLOGIST: Head and Neck Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Scheduled Surveillance Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old West African female, never-smoker, music teacher, who presents for oncology follow-up approximately 6 months after completing definitive concurrent intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) and weekly cisplatin for p16-positive (HPV-associated) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition conventional staging, stage I by HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging criteria). She returns today for review of her most recent biopsy pathology results and ongoing management of post-treatment sequelae. She is eager to discuss removal of her percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Osei-Bonsu initially presented approximately 10 months ago with a 3-month history of progressive right-sided odynophagia and a palpable right neck mass. Workup revealed an exophytic 3.2-cm mass at the right base of tongue with right level IIA nodal metastasis (2.1 cm with internal necrosis on MRI), confirmed on biopsy as moderately differentiated invasive squamous cell carcinoma, p16 strongly positive (>75% of cells, block-like staining), HPV type 16 by RNA in situ hybridization. PET-CT at staging showed SUVmax 14.2 at the primary, 9.8 at the right IIA node, with no distant metastatic disease. NGS revealed a PIK3CA p.E545K activating mutation, TERT promoter mutation, FAT1 nonsense mutation, CCND1 amplification, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion; MSS, TMB low at 6.2 mut/Mb, PD-L1 CPS 12.

The patient was discussed at the head and neck multidisciplinary tumor board and recommended for definitive concurrent chemoradiation. She underwent prophylactic PEG tube placement prior to initiating treatment. She completed IMRT using a simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique: 70 Gy/35 fractions to the high-risk CTV (primary + right IIA node), 63 Gy/35 fractions to the intermediate-risk CTV, and 56 Gy/35 fractions to the low-risk elective nodal regions. Concurrent chemotherapy was given as weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2; she received 6 of 7 planned cycles (week 5 held for grade 2 nephrotoxicity and thrombocytopenia), cumulative delivered dose approximately 414 mg/m2.

Her treatment course was complicated by grade 3 oral mucositis during week 4, requiring a 3-day inpatient admission for IV hydration, pain management (PCA morphine transitioning to extended-release oxycodone), and initiation of PEG tube feeds. She also developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the right neck, grade 2 xerostomia, grade 2 dysgeusia, grade 1 fatigue, and grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine peak 1.3 mg/dL, now recovering). Treatment was completed without further interruptions. She lost a total of 9 kg (12% of pre-treatment body weight) during the treatment course.

At the 3-month post-treatment visit, restaging PET-CT demonstrated complete metabolic response: no FDG-avid disease at the primary site (SUVmax 2.1, physiologic) or the previously involved right level IIA lymph node (previously SUVmax 9.8, now 1.8 with no FDG uptake). MRI neck at that same time showed the right base of tongue mass markedly reduced to 0.9 x 0.7 cm with decreased enhancement, felt to represent post-treatment fibrosis or minimal residual change. Given the PET complete response, MRI near-complete response, and the radiographic uncertainty of the residual 0.9-cm MRI signal, the multidisciplinary team recommended direct laryngoscopy with biopsy approximately 5 months post-treatment for tissue confirmation. This was performed under general anesthesia with narrow-band imaging (NBI) approximately 3-4 weeks ago. Gross inspection showed smooth, post-radiation mucosa at the base of tongue with no visible tumor; random biopsies from the right base of tongue, right lateral pharyngeal wall, right tonsillar fossa, and hypopharynx were taken. Pathology has now returned and is the primary focus of today's visit.

Since the last visit, Ms. Osei-Bonsu reports gradual but meaningful improvement in her overall functional status. She is tolerating a soft mechanical diet orally and estimates that she is meeting approximately 75-80% of her caloric needs by mouth. PEG tube use has been significantly reduced: she is using the tube only overnight for supplemental nutrition and estimates fewer than 25% of daily calories are now coming through the PEG. She is no longer using opioid analgesia. She continues to experience notable xerostomia, which she describes as the most bothersome remaining symptom. She has resumed limited professional activity -- she has been visiting school part-time and is preparing to return to her music teaching position. No new neck masses palpated by the patient. No otalgia, no hemoptysis, no dyspnea.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: Improved energy, weight slowly recovering. No fever, no chills, no drenching night sweats.
- Head/neck/ENT: Persistent xerostomia (dry mouth, worse at night, uses water sips and Biotene sprays frequently). Mild hoarseness, described as stable and unchanged from the prior visit. Odynophagia resolved. No new neck swelling. No otalgia. No facial numbness.
- GI/swallowing: Dysphagia improving. Tolerating soft mechanical diet without significant aspiration events. Occasional coughing on thin liquids (managed by chin-tuck maneuver). No nausea/vomiting. PEG tube site without pain or drainage.
- Respiratory: No cough, no shortness of breath.
- Neurological: No new headaches, no peripheral sensory changes in hands/feet, no hearing complaints reported today (formal audiologic follow-up pending at next visit).
- Endocrine: Reports occasional fatigue and mild constipation since treatment; noted here as possible early hypothyroid symptoms (labs to be checked today).
- Musculoskeletal: Mild stiffness in right neck and limited jaw opening, performing TheraBite jaw exercises at home. No shoulder weakness.
- Psychological: Reports anxiety related to cancer surveillance but describes overall mood as guarded/optimistic. No depressive symptoms meeting formal criteria. She has found support from her church community and partner.

---

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily (hypothyroidism, pre-existing, previously well-controlled)
2. Oxycodone extended-release -- DISCONTINUED at last visit (off opioids for approximately 6 weeks)
3. Magic mouthwash (diphenhydramine/lidocaine/Maalox compounded solution) -- as needed for residual oral discomfort, using infrequently
4. Biotene oral rinse -- multiple times daily for xerostomia
5. Dexamethasone oral rinse 0.1 mg/mL -- tapering, now using once daily at bedtime
6. Ondansetron 4 mg orally as needed -- infrequently used
7. Multivitamin with zinc
8. Calcium carbonate 500 mg twice daily with vitamin D 1000 IU (initiated post-treatment for bone health given nutritional deficiency risk)
9. PEG tube formula: Vital 1.5 at variable rate (currently overnight supplemental only, approximately 500-600 kcal/night)

ALLERGIES: NKDA

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 68 bpm (regular), RR 16 breaths/min, Temperature 36.8 degrees C, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 68 kg (pre-treatment weight 77 kg; at post-treatment nadir approximately 66 kg; current improvement of 2 kg from nadir).

General: Alert, well-appearing, articulate. Thin compared to pre-treatment baseline. Mild voice hoarseness apparent on speech. No acute distress.

Head/Face: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Facial symmetry intact. No trismus at rest; mouth opening on examination approximately 3.7-4.0 cm (mildly reduced from estimated pre-treatment baseline of 4.5 cm, consistent with early radiation-induced trismus).

Oral Cavity / Oropharynx: Oral mucosa: dry, with sparse saliva. No active ulceration. Mild post-radiation mucosal pallor and telangiectatic changes on buccal mucosa and soft palate. Teeth: intact, no evidence of dental caries at this visit. Base of tongue: on direct examination with tongue blade and headlight, smooth fibrotic mucosal contour without visible exophytic lesion, ulceration, or mucosal irregularity. The right tonsil fossa shows expected post-radiation changes (minimal residual lymphoid tissue, smooth epithelialization). No visible tumor.

Neck: Skin of the right neck demonstrates residual post-radiation changes: faint hyperpigmentation and mild induration of the subcutaneous tissues in the prior radiation field, consistent with late radiation fibrosis and dermatitis sequelae. No moist desquamation. Bilateral neck palpation: no palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. The previously palpable right level IIA node is no longer detectable on exam. No supraclavicular or axillary adenopathy. No palpable thyroid nodules; thyroid gland not enlarged.

Flexible Nasopharyngoscopy (performed in clinic today): Nasal passages patent bilaterally. Nasopharynx: no mucosal lesion, eustachian tube orifices clear. Base of tongue: smooth, fibrotic-appearing mucosa at the right base of tongue, no discrete mucosal lesion, no exophytic mass, no ulceration. The region corresponding to the prior tumor site shows expected post-treatment architectural changes without visible neoplasm. Glottic and supraglottic structures: true vocal cords symmetric with mild granular changes of the posterior glottis consistent with radiation/reflux changes; vocal cord mobility grossly intact bilaterally. Hypopharynx: no pooling, no abnormal mucosal lesion. Post-cricoid region: unremarkable. Epiglottis: mildly erythematous but no tumor.

Abdomen: PEG tube in place at left upper quadrant skin, site clean, no erythema, no induration, no signs of infection or granulation. Abdomen soft, non-tender.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No peripheral neuropathy on brief examination. Shoulder shrug symmetric (CN XI intact).

---

LABORATORY AND TEST RESULTS

Today's labs drawn in clinic:
- CBC: WBC 5.4 x10^9/L, Hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, improved from nadir 9.8 g/dL post-treatment), Platelets 228 x10^9/L
- CMP: Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.0 mEq/L, Chloride 102 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 14 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (returned to near-baseline; pre-treatment baseline 0.8 mg/dL), eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2
- Magnesium: 1.8 mEq/L (low-normal; patient counseled on magnesium-rich dietary sources given ongoing risk from prior cisplatin)
- Albumin: 3.4 g/dL (mildly reduced, consistent with ongoing nutritional recovery)
- Prealbumin: 18 mg/dL (slightly below normal; reflects suboptimal but improving nutritional status)
- TSH: 6.8 mIU/L (elevated, reference range 0.4-4.5 mIU/L) -- new finding consistent with radiation-induced hypothyroidism; this exceeds the expected result on her current levothyroxine dose of 75 mcg
- Free T4: 0.9 ng/dL (low-normal)
- LFTs: AST 22, ALT 18, Alkaline Phosphatase 78, Total Bilirubin 0.6 -- all within normal limits

Prior labs / pathology results reviewed today:
- Direct laryngoscopy pathology (approximately 4 weeks ago): Right base of tongue (3 fragments): extensive radiation effect with stromal fibrosis, vascular ectasia, and atypical stromal cells consistent with radiation change; NO VIABLE CARCINOMA IDENTIFIED. Right lateral pharyngeal wall: squamous mucosa with reactive changes, no malignancy. Right tonsillar fossa: benign lymphoid tissue with post-radiation fibrosis, no malignancy. Hypopharynx: squamous mucosa, no malignancy. CONCLUSION: Complete pathologic response to definitive chemoradiation.

Prior imaging reviewed:
- Restaging PET-CT (~10 weeks post-CRT): Complete metabolic response (SUVmax at primary 2.1, physiologic; right IIA node SUVmax 1.8, physiologic). No distant metastatic disease.
- MRI neck (~10 weeks post-CRT): Residual 0.9 x 0.7 cm area of decreased enhancement at right base of tongue, interpreted as post-treatment fibrosis vs. minimal residual tumor. Right IIA node 0.8 cm, no pathologic enhancement. No new adenopathy.

---

ASSESSMENT

1. HPV-positive (p16+, HPV-16) right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma, T2N1M0, stage I (HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging), COMPLETE RESPONSE confirmed pathologically.
Today's visit provides the definitive confirmation we have been awaiting. The direct laryngoscopy biopsies returned showing NO VIABLE CARCINOMA -- extensive radiation effect with fibrosis but zero residual tumor cells in any biopsy specimen from the base of tongue or any sampled oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal site. Combined with the prior PET-CT showing complete metabolic response and the MRI showing a markedly reduced 0.9 cm signal now confirmed to represent pure fibrosis, this represents a complete pathologic response by every criterion. I reviewed these results carefully with Ms. Osei-Bonsu today, emphasizing that this is excellent news. She was visibly emotional and expressed profound relief. I explained that in HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC, particularly in a never-smoker, a complete response to definitive chemoradiation at the 3-6 month post-treatment evaluation is associated with a very favorable long-term prognosis -- estimated 5-year locoregional control rates on the order of 85-90% in this favorable subgroup. I also explained that the residual sub-centimeter MRI signal that prompted the biopsy has now been definitively characterized as benign post-radiation fibrosis, which is reassuring and not unexpected given the intensity of radiation delivered to the primary site.

The primary driver of ongoing surveillance going forward is detection of late local recurrence (uncommon beyond 18-24 months in HPV-positive OPSCC), second primary tumors (particularly given HPV exposure), and late treatment toxicity management. No further oncologic therapy is indicated at this time.

2. Radiation-induced hypothyroidism -- NEW DIAGNOSIS
TSH today is 6.8 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. This is above the upper limit of normal and represents subclinical to overt hypothyroidism likely attributable to radiation injury to the thyroid gland, which is a well-recognized late complication of neck irradiation (particularly radiation fields covering the low neck). Her free T4 is low-normal at 0.9 ng/dL. Her prior TSH was normal prior to treatment on her maintenance levothyroxine dose of 75 mcg. The symptoms she described (fatigue, mild constipation) are consistent with undertreated hypothyroidism. I am increasing her levothyroxine dose from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily, effective today. TSH to be rechecked in 6-8 weeks. She was counseled to take levothyroxine on an empty stomach, at least 30-60 minutes before food and other medications. She understands that lifelong thyroid replacement is likely given radiation-induced thyroid damage.

3. Post-treatment dysphagia and nutritional recovery -- improving
Ms. Osei-Bonsu has made meaningful progress in oral rehabilitation over the past 3 months. She is now meeting approximately 75-80% of caloric needs orally on a soft mechanical diet, with PEG tube providing only supplemental overnight nutrition (estimated <25% of daily calories). Her weight is 68 kg, up approximately 2 kg from nadir but still 9 kg below her pre-treatment weight of 77 kg. Albumin and prealbumin remain mildly depressed, reflecting continued nutritional recovery. Dietetics was contacted today (telephone consultation) and agrees that oral intake is adequate to proceed with PEG removal planning when oral caloric intake consistently exceeds 1800-2000 kcal/day for 1-2 weeks. She will be seen by speech-language pathology next week for formal FEES reassessment to evaluate swallowing safety and guide dietary texture advancement. If the FEES is reassuring and oral intake targets are met over the next 4-6 weeks, we will plan PEG removal with gastroenterology. The patient is strongly motivated to have the PEG removed, which I view as a positive indicator of her functional recovery.

4. Persistent xerostomia -- grade 1-2
Continued significant complaint. Saliva production remains markedly reduced compared to pre-treatment baseline. She is using Biotene and frequent water sips with good behavioral coping. I discussed the option of initiating pilocarpine (a muscarinic agonist, sialagogue) for xerostomia, explaining that it may provide modest but meaningful benefit for some patients. She agreed to try pilocarpine 5 mg three times daily with meals, with a goal of increasing salivary flow. Common side effects (diaphoresis, flushing, rhinorrhea, urinary frequency) reviewed. She will trial for 4-6 weeks and report back. Dental hygiene counseled extensively: daily fluoride trays, xylitol gum/lozenges, and bi-annual dental care are strongly recommended given the risk of radiation caries. She has an appointment with her dentist next month.

5. Radiation-induced trismus -- mild, improving with therapy
Mouth opening measured at approximately 3.7-4.0 cm today, mildly below her estimated pre-treatment baseline of approximately 4.5 cm. She has been performing TheraBite jaw-stretching exercises at home and reports gradual improvement. I encouraged her to continue daily jaw stretching exercises, ideally 3-4 sets of 20 repetitions per day. No intervention beyond physical therapy is needed at this time. Physical medicine and rehabilitation consult to be considered if trismus worsens or does not continue to improve.

6. Mild anemia
Hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL, normocytic. This is improved from the post-treatment nadir of approximately 9.8 g/dL and is consistent with slow recovery from combined chemoradiation-related bone marrow suppression and nutritional deficiency. Iron studies and B12/folate not checked today but were recently within normal limits at the 3-month visit; will recheck at next visit if hemoglobin does not continue trending up. No transfusion indicated. She is on a daily multivitamin.

7. Mild hypomagnesemia (1.8 mEq/L)
Persistent mild hypomagnesemia, a recognized late sequela of cisplatin therapy (cisplatin causes renal tubular magnesium wasting that can persist for months to years after treatment). Creatinine is now back to near-baseline (0.9), so renal function is recovering. Oral magnesium supplementation (magnesium oxide 400 mg daily) initiated, with instruction on GI tolerance (loose stools at higher doses). Will recheck at next visit.

---

PLAN

1. Complete pathologic response confirmed -- excellent news. No additional oncologic therapy needed. Surveillance plan per NCCN HPV-positive OPSCC guidelines:
   - Clinical exam (flexible nasopharyngoscopy included) every 1-3 months for the first year post-treatment completion; we are now at 6 months, next scheduled visit in 3 months (9 months post-treatment)
   - Restaging imaging: PET-CT planned at 9-12 months post-treatment (next major imaging checkpoint)
   - Annual thyroid function tests indefinitely (radiation-induced hypothyroidism now documented)
   - Dental follow-up every 6 months with fluoride treatment
   - Continue speech-language pathology quarterly

2. Increase levothyroxine from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily for radiation-induced hypothyroidism. Recheck TSH and free T4 in 6-8 weeks (can be done by her primary care physician).

3. PEG tube removal planning: FEES (functional endoscopic evaluation of swallowing) with SLP next week. If swallowing is safe and oral intake targets maintained (>1800-2000 kcal/day orally, weight stable), proceed with PEG removal by gastroenterology in 4-6 weeks.

4. Initiate pilocarpine 5 mg TID with meals for xerostomia. Re-evaluate benefit/tolerability at next visit.

5. Initiate magnesium oxide 400 mg daily for hypomagnesemia. Recheck Mg at next visit.

6. Continue TheraBite jaw exercises for mild trismus.

7. Nutritional follow-up with dietetics at next visit to reassess caloric intake and guide transition to full oral diet.

8. Audiology follow-up in 3 months to assess for cisplatin-related ototoxicity (high-frequency SNHL monitoring).

9. Skin/dermatology: Post-radiation neck skin hyperpigmentation noted; patient counseled on sun protection (SPF 50+ to neck), moisturization. No intervention needed at this time.

10. Psychosocial support: Patient continues to cope well overall. Social work and/or survivorship counseling available if needed. Referred to the institutional cancer survivorship program for comprehensive late-effects monitoring.

Total visit time: approximately 50 minutes. Greater than 50% of time spent in counseling, discussion of results, and care coordination.

Return to clinic: 3 months (9 months post-treatment completion), with PET-CT to be scheduled for that time point.
